---
title: "Merging EFLALO and TACSAT: Technical Documentation"
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```


**Needs updating**

### Overview

The merge process links VMS ping records (TACSAT) to logbook records (EFLALO) in two
phases: (1) assigning a trip identifier to each ping via
`vmstools::mergeEflalo2Tacsat()`, then (2) enriching matched pings with the logbook
attributes recorded for that trip, first by a coarse trip-level match and then by
`trip_assign()` for trips with within-trip variation. A proposed dplyr-based alternative
that resolves the key limitation is described at the end.

---

### Phase 1 — Trip Identifier Assignment

**`mergeEflalo2Tacsat(eflalo, tacsat)`** is the entry point for this phase. It returns
the full TACSAT data frame with one new column, `FT_REF`, whose value is the matched
logbook trip identifier or `0` where no match was found.

**Step 1 — Datetime construction.**
If not already present, POSIXct timestamps are constructed from raw character
fields.[^1] For EFLALO: `FT_DDAT` + `FT_DTIME` → `D_DATIM` (departure) and
`FT_LDAT` + `FT_LTIME` → `L_DATIM` (return). For TACSAT: `SI_DATE` + `SI_TIME`
→ `SI_DATIM`. All are parsed under `"%d/%m/%Y %H:%M"` in the GMT timezone.

**Step 2 — Type coercion and sorting.**
`VE_REF` is coerced to character in both datasets. Both are then sorted
chronologically within vessel: EFLALO by `VE_REF`, `D_DATIM`, `L_DATIM`; TACSAT by
`VE_REF`, `SI_DATIM`.

**Step 3 — Split by vessel.**
Both datasets are split into named lists keyed by `VE_REF`. `pmatch` maps each
unique TACSAT vessel to its EFLALO counterpart, producing an index vector
(`tacefmatch`) of length equal to the number of distinct TACSAT vessels.

**Step 4 — Interval join (per vessel).**
For each vessel the function:

1. Extracts unique trip windows from EFLALO — one row per distinct `FT_REF`,
   retaining `D_DATIM`, `L_DATIM`, and `FT_REF`.
2. Computes two outer difference matrices: `smdtime` (ping time − departure;
   non-negative means ping is on or after departure) and `gtltime` (return − ping
   time; non-negative means ping is before or at return).[^2]
3. Identifies, for each trip, the first ping index satisfying the departure
   condition (`st`) and the last satisfying the return condition (`en`).
4. Retains only pairs where `st ≤ en`. `FT_REF` is initialised to `0` for all pings;
   where a valid `[st, en]` interval exists, `mapply(seq, st, en)` assigns the
   trip's `FT_REF`. Vessels absent from EFLALO receive `FT_REF = 0` throughout.

After the loop the split TACSAT list is reassembled into a single data frame with
the new `FT_REF` column.

---

### Phase 2 — Attribute Enrichment

**Step 5 — Quality check and filtering.**
Records with `FT_REF == 0` are separated into `tacsatpmin` and saved to disk. The
proportion is reported; a high value signals mismatched date/time formats between
datasets. Only pings with `FT_REF != 0` are carried forward into `tacsatp`.

**Step 6 — Trip-level attribute assignment (coarse).**
A vectorised `match(tacsatp$FT_REF, eflalo$FT_REF)` copies the following EFLALO
columns to each ping in `tacsatp`: `LE_GEAR`, `LE_MSZ`, `VE_LEN`, `VE_KW`,
`LE_RECT`, `LE_MET`, `LE_WIDTH`, `VE_FLT`, `VE_COU`. Because `match` returns the
first matching row, this produces correct results only when the column value is
constant across all logbook events of the trip.[^3]

**Step 7 — Within-trip, event-level attribute refinement.**
`trip_assign(tacsatp, eflalo, col, haul_logbook)` is called once per column for
`LE_GEAR`, `LE_MSZ`, `LE_RECT`, `LE_MET`, and (if present in EFLALO) `LE_WIDTH`.
It scopes its work to trips that contain **more than one distinct value** of the
target column; all other trips are already fully resolved by Step 6 and are left
untouched.[^4]

For the selected trips the existing column value is cleared from the TACSAT subset,
then assignment proceeds through up to three ordered passes:

- **Pass 1 — Log-event date matching.** EFLALO events are aggregated by
  `(FT_REF, LE_CDAT)`. Only day-trip combinations where the column value is
  unambiguous (a single unique value on that date within that trip) contribute. A
  left join then matches each ping where `SI_DATE == LE_CDAT`. Pings whose date is
  ambiguous or has no logbook event receive `NA`.

- **Pass 2 — Nearest-haul datetime matching (optional).** Runs only when
  `haul_logbook = TRUE` and `LE_SDATTIM` is present in EFLALO. A rolling
  nearest-time join (`data.table roll = "nearest"`) against haul start and end
  times assigns the column value from the temporally closest haul event within the
  trip.[^5]

- **Pass 3 — Catch-weighted fallback.** For any pings still carrying `NA`, the
  column value associated with the highest summed `LE_KG_TOT` within the trip is
  assigned to all remaining unresolved pings. If `LE_KG_TOT` is absent it is
  computed as the row-wise sum of all `LE_KG_*` columns. This aggregation and
  selection is done **independently for each column** in separate `trip_assign`
  calls.

Each `trip_assign` call returns only the affected trips. The caller splices them
back into `tacsatp` by dropping those trips and row-binding the revised sub-frame
(`rbindlist(..., fill = TRUE)`). This splice-and-replace is repeated for each
column in sequence.

---

### Assumption About the LE_ Variable Combinations Matrix

The code treats `LE_GEAR`, `LE_MSZ`, `LE_RECT`, `LE_MET`, and `LE_WIDTH` as
**independently assignable**. Each `trip_assign` call handles exactly one column
at a time with no reference to what was assigned in any prior call. The implicit
assumption is:

> **The joint combination `(gear, mesh, rect, metier, width)` for a ping can be
> correctly reconstructed column by column. The optimal value for each column is
> independent of the optimal value for any other column.**

This holds only when each calendar day within a trip has exactly one logbook event,
making all columns simultaneously unambiguous and drawn from the same source row.

---

### Cases the Process Handles Correctly

| Case | Why it works |
|---|---|
| One logbook event per day per trip | Pass 1 assigns all columns from the same source row simultaneously; all joint combinations are preserved |
| Multiple events per day, all columns vary, catch weight consistent | Pass 3 picks the same dominant event for every column, yielding an internally consistent combination |
| `haul_logbook = TRUE` with `LE_SDATTIM` present | Pass 2 narrows assignment temporally before the catch-weight fallback, reducing Pass 3 cases |

---

### Cases the Process Does Not Handle Correctly

The setup chunk below is required for all three runnable examples.

```r
library(dplyr)
library(data.table)

# Custom NOT-IN operator (defined in 0_global.R)
'%!in%' <- function(x, y) !('%in%'(x, y))

# trip_assign is defined in 0_global.R — source it before running these examples:
# source("path/to/0_global.R")
```

---

#### Case 1 — Minority combination permanently lost

When a trip has two coherent combinations on the same day and all columns are
ambiguous, Pass 3 selects each column's dominant value independently. If those
dominant values happen to co-occur they form one consistent combination; the other
combination is never assigned to any ping regardless of its catch weight.

```r
# EFLALO: trip T01, one day, two events — both columns ambiguous.
eflalo_c1 <- tribble(
  ~FT_REF, ~LE_CDAT,     ~LE_GEAR, ~LE_RECT, ~LE_KG_TOT,
  "T01",   "09/03/2024", "OTB",    "41G9",    800,
  "T01",   "09/03/2024", "GNS",    "41H0",    200
)

# TACSAT: three pings on 09/03, coarse match (Step 6) gave first eflalo row.
tacsatp_c1 <- tribble(
  ~FT_REF, ~SI_DATE,     ~VE_REF, ~LE_GEAR, ~LE_RECT,
  "T01",   "09/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41G9",
  "T01",   "09/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41G9",
  "T01",   "09/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41G9"
)

# Step 7 — trip_assign for LE_GEAR
# Day 09/03 has OTB and GNS → ambiguous → all pings NA after Pass 1 → Pass 3.
# Trip total: OTB = 800, GNS = 200 → OTB wins.
result_gear <- trip_assign(tacsatp_c1, eflalo_c1, col = "LE_GEAR", haul_logbook = FALSE)
result_gear |> select(FT_REF, SI_DATE, LE_GEAR)
# All three pings: LE_GEAR = OTB

# Splice back
tacsatp_c1 <- rbindlist(
  list(tacsatp_c1[tacsatp_c1$FT_REF %!in% result_gear$FT_REF, ], result_gear),
  fill = TRUE
)

# Step 7 — trip_assign for LE_RECT
# Day 09/03 has 41G9 and 41H0 → ambiguous → all pings NA after Pass 1 → Pass 3.
# Trip total: 41G9 = 800, 41H0 = 200 → 41G9 wins.
result_rect <- trip_assign(tacsatp_c1, eflalo_c1, col = "LE_RECT", haul_logbook = FALSE)
result_rect |> select(FT_REF, SI_DATE, LE_GEAR, LE_RECT)
# All three pings: LE_GEAR = OTB, LE_RECT = 41G9
#
# The combination (GNS, 41H0) — 200 kg, a real logbook event — is never
# assigned to any ping. It is permanently absent from the output.
```

---

#### Case 2 — Phantom combination from independent column fallbacks

When pings fall on a day where one column is unambiguous (resolved by Pass 1) but
another is ambiguous (deferred to Pass 3), and the Pass 3 fallback for the ambiguous
column is dominated by a different gear's activity on a different day, the resulting
combination may never have appeared in the logbook.

```r
# EFLALO: trip T02.
#   Day 05/03: OTB fished in two rectangles (41G9 = 100 kg, 41F8 = 50 kg).
#   Day 06/03: GNS fished in one rectangle (41H0 = 500 kg) — a completely
#              separate gear in a completely separate rectangle.
eflalo_c2 <- tribble(
  ~FT_REF, ~LE_CDAT,     ~LE_GEAR, ~LE_RECT, ~LE_KG_TOT,
  "T02",   "05/03/2024", "OTB",    "41G9",    100,
  "T02",   "05/03/2024", "OTB",    "41F8",     50,
  "T02",   "06/03/2024", "GNS",    "41H0",    500
)

# TACSAT: two pings on 05/03 (OTB day). No pings on 06/03 (e.g., port day).
# Coarse match (Step 6) gave first eflalo row values: OTB / 41G9.
tacsatp_c2 <- tribble(
  ~FT_REF, ~SI_DATE,     ~VE_REF, ~LE_GEAR, ~LE_RECT,
  "T02",   "05/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41G9",
  "T02",   "05/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41G9"
)

# Step 7 — trip_assign for LE_GEAR
# Day 05/03: only OTB → unambiguous → OTB assigned via Pass 1. Correct.
result_gear2 <- trip_assign(tacsatp_c2, eflalo_c2, col = "LE_GEAR", haul_logbook = FALSE)
result_gear2 |> select(FT_REF, SI_DATE, LE_GEAR)
# Both pings: LE_GEAR = OTB  ✓

tacsatp_c2 <- rbindlist(
  list(tacsatp_c2[tacsatp_c2$FT_REF %!in% result_gear2$FT_REF, ], result_gear2),
  fill = TRUE
)

# Step 7 — trip_assign for LE_RECT
# Day 05/03: 41G9 and 41F8 → ambiguous → NA after Pass 1 → Pass 3.
# Trip totals: 41G9 = 100, 41F8 = 50, 41H0 = 500.
# 41H0 wins — driven entirely by GNS activity on 06/03.
# OTB never visited 41H0 in the logbook.
result_rect2 <- trip_assign(tacsatp_c2, eflalo_c2, col = "LE_RECT", haul_logbook = FALSE)
result_rect2 |> select(FT_REF, SI_DATE, LE_GEAR, LE_RECT)
# Both pings: LE_GEAR = OTB, LE_RECT = 41H0
#
# LE_RECT = 41H0 is a phantom: no logbook row records OTB in 41H0.
# The assignment was driven by GNS catch on a different day inflating 41H0's
# trip-level total in the independent Pass 3 aggregation for LE_RECT.
```

---

#### Case 3 — Correct column contaminated by cross-day activity

When one column is unambiguous on a day (resolved correctly by Pass 1) but another
column is ambiguous on the same day (deferred to Pass 3), the Pass 3 aggregation
uses trip-level totals that include other days and other gears. This can cause
pings to be assigned a rectangle whose selection was driven by a different gear's
activity on a different day — overriding the within-day signal.

```r
# EFLALO: trip T03.
#   Day 07/03: OTB fished in two rectangles (41H0 = 300 kg, 41G9 = 200 kg).
#              On this day, 41H0 was the dominant OTB rectangle (300 > 200).
#   Day 08/03: GNS fished in 41G9 (400 kg), inflating 41G9's trip total.
eflalo_c3 <- tribble(
  ~FT_REF, ~LE_CDAT,     ~LE_GEAR, ~LE_RECT, ~LE_KG_TOT,
  "T03",   "07/03/2024", "OTB",    "41H0",    300,
  "T03",   "07/03/2024", "OTB",    "41G9",    200,
  "T03",   "08/03/2024", "GNS",    "41G9",    400
)

# TACSAT: two pings on 07/03. Coarse match gave first eflalo row: OTB / 41H0.
tacsatp_c3 <- tribble(
  ~FT_REF, ~SI_DATE,     ~VE_REF, ~LE_GEAR, ~LE_RECT,
  "T03",   "07/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41H0",
  "T03",   "07/03/2024", "V01",   "OTB",    "41H0"
)

# Step 7 — trip_assign for LE_GEAR
# Day 07/03: only OTB → unambiguous → OTB via Pass 1.  Correct.
result_gear3 <- trip_assign(tacsatp_c3, eflalo_c3, col = "LE_GEAR", haul_logbook = FALSE)
result_gear3 |> select(FT_REF, SI_DATE, LE_GEAR)
# Both pings: LE_GEAR = OTB  ✓

tacsatp_c3 <- rbindlist(
  list(tacsatp_c3[tacsatp_c3$FT_REF %!in% result_gear3$FT_REF, ], result_gear3),
  fill = TRUE
)

# Step 7 — trip_assign for LE_RECT
# Day 07/03: 41H0 and 41G9 → ambiguous → NA after Pass 1 → Pass 3.
# Trip totals: 41H0 = 300, 41G9 = 200 + 400 = 600.
# 41G9 wins — but the 400 kg contribution comes from GNS on 08/03,
# not from OTB on 07/03. Within day 07/03, OTB's dominant rectangle was 41H0.
result_rect3 <- trip_assign(tacsatp_c3, eflalo_c3, col = "LE_RECT", haul_logbook = FALSE)
result_rect3 |> select(FT_REF, SI_DATE, LE_GEAR, LE_RECT)
# Both pings: LE_GEAR = OTB, LE_RECT = 41G9
#
# LE_RECT = 41G9 is not a phantom (OTB did visit 41G9 on 07/03, 200 kg),
# but it is the wrong choice: 41H0 was the higher-catch OTB rectangle that day.
# The 41G9 assignment was driven by GNS catch on 08/03 contaminating the
# trip-level aggregation used in the independent Pass 3 call for LE_RECT.
```

---

### Alternative: dplyr Join Skeleton (Preserves Joint Combinations)

This alternative assumes EFLALO has been decomposed into two tables:

- `trips` — one row per trip; holds `VE_` and `FT_` variables (`vid`, `tid`,
  `T1`, `T2`, vessel characteristics).
- `events` — one row per logbook event; holds `LE_` variables (`tid`, `date`,
  `LE_GEAR`, `LE_MSZ`, `LE_RECT`, `LE_MET`, `LE_WIDTH`, `LE_KG_TOT`, …).

```r
# Step 1 — assign trip to each ping via interval join
tacsat <- tacsat |>
  left_join(trips, by = join_by(vid, T1 <= time, T2 >= time))

# Step 2 — assign all LE_ variables jointly from the matching event row
tacsat <- tacsat |>
  left_join(events, by = join_by(tid, ping_date == date))

# Step 3 — fallback: for pings with no date-matching event,
#           use the single highest-catch event for the trip
dominant_event <- events |>
  slice_max(LE_KG_TOT, by = tid, with_ties = FALSE)

tacsat <- tacsat |>
  left_join(dominant_event, by = "tid", suffix = c("", "_fallback")) |>
  mutate(
    LE_GEAR = coalesce(LE_GEAR, LE_GEAR_fallback),
    LE_RECT = coalesce(LE_RECT, LE_RECT_fallback),
    # ... remaining LE_ columns
  )
```

**What this resolves.** All `LE_` variables are drawn from the same event row in
Step 2, so cross-column inconsistency and phantom combinations cannot arise. The
fallback in Step 3 also preserves joint coherence — all unresolved pings receive the
same dominant event row rather than independently chosen column values.

**Residual open issue.** Step 2 produces duplicate ping rows when a single calendar
day within a trip has more than one logbook event (the many-to-many case). This
requires an explicit deduplication or pre-aggregation strategy — the same problem
`trip_assign` sidesteps by collapsing to one value per `(FT_REF, LE_CDAT)` before
joining, at the cost of the column-independence limitation described above.[^7]

---

### Footnotes

[^1]: The conditional checks (`if(!"D_DATIM" %in% colnames(eflalo2))`) mean the
function is safe to call on data that has already been partially prepared, but also
that silently pre-existing timestamp columns will be used as-is without format
validation. Confirm formats match `"%d/%m/%Y %H:%M"` before calling.

[^2]: The outer difference approach is equivalent to a non-equi interval join but
computed as dense matrices in base R. Memory cost scales as O(n_pings × n_trips)
per vessel. For vessels with many trips and many pings this can become large; the
split-by-vessel structure mitigates this but does not eliminate it.

[^3]: Because `match` returns the **first** matching row index in `eflalo`, the
value assigned depends on the row order of `eflalo`. If `eflalo` is not sorted
consistently this can introduce non-deterministic behaviour for multi-event trips.

[^4]: For `LE_MET`, `trip_assign` additionally restricts the distinct-value count
to members of the `valid_metiers` vector, which is loaded from the ICES RCG metier
reference list in `0_global.R` via `metier_table$Metier_level6`. Trips where all
metier values are invalid (e.g. all `NA` or unrecognised codes) will not be flagged
as multi-value and will pass through with whatever Step 6 assigned.

[^5]: Pass 2 constructs `meantime` from both haul start (`LE_SDATTIM`) and haul end
(`LE_EDATTIM`), stacks them with `rbind`, then uses `data.table`'s rolling join
(`roll = "nearest"`). The result is that each unmatched ping is assigned to the
nearest haul boundary in time, regardless of whether that boundary is a start or
end. This can assign a ping to a haul that had already finished or not yet started
if the gap is large.

[^6]: Whether the output combination is "phantom" in any given run depends on the
exact catch totals. The problem is structural: because the two `trip_assign` calls
for `LE_GEAR` and `LE_RECT` operate on separate copies of `tacsatp` and make
independent decisions, there is no guarantee that the selected values originate from
the same source row, even when a single coherent dominant event exists.

[^7]: The many-to-many issue in Step 2 of the dplyr skeleton can be handled by
pre-aggregating events to one row per `(tid, date)` before joining — for example,
taking the event with the highest `LE_KG_TOT` on each day within each trip. This is
equivalent to a spatially and temporally aware version of what `trip_assign` does in
Pass 1, but applied jointly to all columns at once, preserving their co-occurrence.
